CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
lexical_adapted: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a bodily quality to red that speaks uniquely with the human mind. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, modern artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” gathered personal memories through a collection of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the giver describing lost people and past moments. Almost four miles of red thread wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, calling to mind the artist’s attention to the basic ties that connect humans to one another and the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
lexical_adapted: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or carefully mixed colors, there’s a human urge to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the places we live in, and the spaces we build. Green brings to mind rich landscapes, growth and success, and even health and well-being. Learn about how green has been made and understood across different cultures and traditions shown in the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
lexical_adapted: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to copy shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
lexical_adapted: The green coating on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper wears away. Coating occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of wear products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also many scattered small patches of dull red copper ore, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
lexical_adapted: Copper corrosion products can be used to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
lexical_adapted:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the base of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow bits of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
lexical_adapted: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been taken from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can show a wide range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
lexical_adapted: Jade was the first true luxury material to appear in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully patterned stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Made of interlocked, fibrous crystals and found as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials mixed in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to keep its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
lexical_adapted: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was sick near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and healing powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. In the end, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
lexical_adapted: Green is so common in the plant life of the natural world that the color easily brings to mind rich environments full of plants. Life, growth, and renewal are all ideas that come to mind in the context of green landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, building cities required carving out spaces from the green, tropical environment. The jungle had to be carefully managed; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully cared for. When population centers moved due to political, environmental, and religious changes, the tropical landscape easily spread over the built environment, turning once city landscapes back into jungle. This environment influenced Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curl, and sprays of leaves change surfaces into rich landscapes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
lexical_adapted: Set in a garden, the image shows a group of angels, called huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood matches other Persian pictures of outdoor feasts or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing shows heavenly creatures--a favorite subject in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
lexical_adapted: Referred to as peri winged heavenly creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The beautifully made composition shows a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is clear in the tiny designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of depth and texture to the form of this heavenly being.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
lexical_adapted: Four-part inro in shape of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably taken from the coin called ryo). The inside rim of each of the three sections fits into the base of the section above it or into the lid. The four parts are held by a blue-green silk cord going through two holes in each of the pieces, tied in a bow below and held by the bead (ojime) above and attached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
lexical_adapted: Box, small round, with cover of same size as the box itself. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: round design of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.

The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been called egg-shell ware. The material is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an unpainted background. CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
lexical_adapted: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Supportive critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler showed it in London in 1884. One said, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever made by an artist who has learned how to see….It is without detail, without apparent work, without dramatic interest; but it is beautiful in color, faultless in tone, and its well-thought-out mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
lexical_adapted: Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: light brown. Glaze: clear over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock among flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the outside. Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
lexical_adapted: Molded Yue-ware containers like this were made in large amounts, but their use is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled inside. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with overly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
lexical_adapted: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, leaf scroll and scattered flower heads, each with a leaf or shell "symbol" mark on base. Part of a five-piece set with three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
lexical_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall comes down in branching small streams over a steep, rock face. In the front, pilgrims go up from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist god linked with kindness. The faraway and high-up feel of this scene is highlighted by the bands of clouds at the lower left.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
lexical_adapted: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: light brown pottery. Glaze: clear glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large uneven cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Inside of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow outward-turned rim. The shape of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the form of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
lexical_adapted:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that make up the Khamsa, tells the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known picture copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most deeply expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Unhappy with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has gone back to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw many times tries to see Shirin, who finally gives in to his request. Protected by the royal umbrella and standing on costly brocades and carpets, the king makes gestures in begging toward Shirin, who looks down from a window in her castle. Although the text says that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has placed the reconciliation in a rich garden setting softly lit by a fading moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking likeness to known pictures of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
lexical_adapted: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was first called The Morning Call. The woman shown in the mirror on the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was linked to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a believable illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler used a color scheme and sharply angled viewpoint that highlight the flatness of the picture surface.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
lexical_adapted: Popular tales and lively images played a role in passing on beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a story that tells of the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to faraway lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and lively painting, the artist shows the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The writings spread across the painting, which repeatedly name parts of the ship, show a fascination with sea travel. The picture of the Union Jack gives a sense of closeness to a story that took place long before the British came to India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
lexical_adapted: Funeral vase: tall, thin, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, clear green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: holy figures and a dragon holding the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.

CuratorialText: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known illustrated version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great martial prowess, was transformed in popular literature into a legendary hero who rescued the realm from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the population, dining on locals and holding young maidens captive. With divine assistance and various means of subterfuge, Yorimitsu beheaded the ogre and returned the region to tranquility. Kyosai's interest in the narrative format and his careful connoisseurship of ancient works of art are evident in his paintings and sketchbooks. Riddled with macabre humor and images of the grotesque, his own paintings catered to the tastes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.
lexical_adapted: The Tale of Shuten Doji has long been popular in Japan; the earliest-known pictured version of this tale dates to the fourteenth century. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (948-1021), a historical figure of great fighting skill, was changed in popular literature into a legendary hero who saved the land from demonic forces. Shuten Doji, a monster who lived southwest of Kyoto, terrorized the people, eating locals and holding young women captive. With godly help and various ways of trickery, Yorimitsu cut off the ogre's head and returned the area to peace. Kyosai's interest in the story form and his careful study of old works of art are clear in his paintings and sketchbooks. Full of dark humor and images of the ugly, his own paintings appealed to the likes of late Edo (1615-1868) and early Meiji (1868-1912) audiences.

CuratorialText: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, completing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but recalls a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, emphasizing the geometrical patterns created by the play of variously colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light splayed across the near foreground.
lexical_adapted: Hassam visited Oregon during the summer of 1908, finishing this view of Portland's Chinatown after his return to New York. The subject matter is unusual for Hassam, but brings to mind a series of shop-front scenes painted by Whistler in the 1880s. Like Whistler, Hassam treated the street as a shallow stage set, highlighting the geometrical patterns created by the play of differently colored rectangular shaped-bricks, signs, windows, doors, walls, sidewalk, alley, and the white diagonal of broken light spread across the near foreground.

CuratorialText: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.
lexical_adapted: Tall, blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with long neck, round bulbous body and "garlic"-shaped neck/mouth. Round ring base, slightly uneven. The body decorated with scrolling, flowering vines and animals.

CuratorialText: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unrequited love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The ornate pictorial decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which employ powders and particles of gold and other material to create intricate designs.
lexical_adapted: Legendary and theatrical references abound in the decoration of the cover of this lacquer box for an inkstone, a water dropper, and writing brushes. The Wisteria Maiden (Fuji Musume), whose silvery facial features have oxidized to bluish black, is a common subject of Otsu-e, a type of folk painting sold in the town of Otsu near Lake Biwa. In nineteenth-century kabuki, the Wisteria Maiden became the focus of a dance expressing unreturned love to the vocal and instrumental music of a nagauta (literally, "long song"). With her is a praying demon, another figure often seen in Otsu-e. The Thunder God rushes down from above, beating his ring of drums as the couple takes shelter from a rainstorm. The fancy picture decoration of this box was created by an artist who had mastered maki-e techniques, which use powders and particles of gold and other material to create detailed designs.

CuratorialText: Bowl, conical, flaring; deep foot with four silica spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, sonant. Glaze: gray-green celadon; crackled in long lines. Decoration: molded and incised in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and foliage filling sides: single line incised below rim.
lexical_adapted: Bowl, cone-shaped, spreading out; deep foot with four glass spur-marks underneath on base inside footrim. Six notches in rim. Clay: dense, ringing. Glaze: gray-green celadon; cracked in long lines. Decoration: molded and carved in paste under glaze: rosette in bottom; hibiscus flowers and leaves filling sides: single line carved below rim.

CuratorialText: Depicts a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a dappled horse with trappings and a saddle. Spurious inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.
lexical_adapted: Shows a hilly landscape, with a scraggly tree, and a Central Asian groom in a red jacket holding the bridle of a spotted horse with decorations and a saddle. False inscription and one seal, no colophons or labels.

CuratorialText: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines depicting a bird perched on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.
lexical_adapted: A rectangular, off-white frit tile with an opaque white underglaze. Overglaze painted with yellow, green, turquoise, blue and manganese black/purple outlines showing a bird sitting on a fruit tree branch. Sides and back are unglazed.

CuratorialText: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where spectators are enthralled by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.
lexical_adapted: Painting is one of a series of eight views of Tokyo by Ogata Gekko. This view of a racetrack focuses on the action in the foreground, where viewers are excited by the competition. Contained in modern wooden box with lid.

CuratorialText: One of a pair of blue and white vases, Kangxi period, each of trumpet shape form with flared foot, after a bronze Ku, each of the three sections with large loose lotus scroll, the central lobed section encircled by a diaper filled band.

CuratorialText: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear form, on a high spreading foot; plaster restorations including handle and foot. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly disintegrated and having a silver iridescence.
lexical_adapted: Pitcher, of a full-bodied pear shape, on a high spreading base; plaster repairs including handle and base. Clay: soft, sandy, white. Glaze: turquoise green, partly broken down and having a silver shimmer.

CuratorialText: Bowl, deep ovoidal; low foot with kiln-slag attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: brilliant olive-gray, finely crackled. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large floral motif (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, difficult to decipher under glaze.
lexical_adapted: Bowl, deep egg-shaped; low foot with kiln-waste attachments. Clay: hard. Glaze: shiny olive-gray, finely cracked. Decoration: molded in the paste in light relief, under glaze; single large flower design (peony?) filling bottom, lower half of walls, hard to make out under glaze.

CuratorialText: Clay: dense, resonant, buff stoneware. Glaze: transparent, crackled, over white slip; unglazed area stained blackish-brown. Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: linear patterns representing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on underside of lid.

Decoration: in cobalt blue, under glaze: line patterns showing maple leaves and water or pampas grass; geometric bands on sides of lid and base. Square seal on bottom of lid.

CuratorialText: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with bulbous lower body, cylindrical center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outflaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.
lexical_adapted: Tall blue and white glazed porcelain vase, with round lower body, straight center body with pinched "snake" handles and slightly outward-flaring rim. Porcelain body with dark blue cobalt underglaze with gray-blue-white glaze. Floral decorative patterns.

CuratorialText: Dish, low, with rounded sides; one chip on rim; hair-line crack. Clay: white porcelain. Glaze: clear, feldspathic; a few pinholes and impurity flecks. Center inside, "three friends of winter" (pine, plum, and bamboo) with garden rock, and fungus, enclosed by double line band which is repeated at rim. Outside, following model, a scene of figures on a garden terrace with plants and trees.

Five spur marks (from clay wads) on foot rim.

CuratorialText: Bowl, small, ovoidal, on a bold, spreading foot. Cracked and repaired. Clay: soft, grayish-white. Glaze: inside, a deep aubergine; outside now covered with an iridescent patina. Decoration: painted in black under glaze.

CuratorialText: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are constellations of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is composed of seven stars while Centaurus, whose forepart is made up of a man and hindpart of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."
lexical_adapted: Both Corvus, "The Raven," and Centaurus, "The Centaur," are star groups of the southern hemisphere. Corvus is made of seven stars while Centaurus, whose front part is made up of a man and back part of a horse, consists of 37. Al-Kazwini records that the last two stars of Centaurus are called "the two sworn upon," and according to tradition one of these passes very close to the path of Corvus. Some people, writes al-Kazwini, believe that the star belongs to "the Raven" while others insist that it is part of "The Centaur."

CuratorialText: Bowl, large, deep, ovoidal; heavy foot; cracked. Clay: heavy, coarse, gray. Glaze: white, with tinges of green; crackled and discolored to a brownish tone. Chipped along the rim. Decoration: painted in blue and black under glaze.

CuratorialText: Black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress-wood utensil stand (shin daisu) with four black-lacquered posts and two shelves; four black-lacquered (shin nuri) cypress boards (shikiita) of various sizes for use with formal tea procedures.

CuratorialText: Vessel in the shape of a Shang dynasty ceremonial "ku;" copper decorated with cloisonne enamels in light blue, dark blue, green, red, yellow, and white; inside the foot is an inscription of eight characters in relief giving a K'ang-hsi date. A separate flower holder fits inside the mouth. During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of ancient objects as well as contemporary ones made in an archaistic style was considered a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, reproduce aspects of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.

During the Qing dynasty (1644-1911), the display of old objects as well as modern ones made in an old-fashioned style was seen as a sign of learning and refined taste. The shape and decoration of this vase, including the mask-like faces on the base, copy parts of a Bronze Age ritual vessel called a gu.

CuratorialText: Polychrome. Clay: fine white porcelain. Glaze: transparent, high fired. Decoration: dragons amid clouds incised in the paste and covered with brilliant green enamel; six-character Hung-chih mark on base. (Pair with F1952.20).

CuratorialText: Bulb-bowl: circular, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of bosses. Incised numeral 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside:olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.
lexical_adapted: Bulb-bowl: round, on three cloud-scroll feet. Two rows of raised bumps. Cut number 1 (one) and spur-marks on base. Clay: hard, fine, light-gray. Glaze: inside: olive, strong lavender-blue and lighter green-blue; outside: rose-purple, blue, gray and olive.

CuratorialText:
The brilliant paintings on this pair of folding screens are regarded as masterpieces among only six surviving sets of screens by Sotatsu, a talented and innovative artist who headed a painting workshop known as Tawaraya. As a townsman, Sotatsu produced paintings such as fans for popular consumption. By the late 1620s, however, Sotatsu was painting for the imperial court, and his works survive in the collections of the Kyoto imperial palace. For his artistic merit, he was granted the honorary Buddhist ecclesiastical title Hokkyo (Bridge of the Law), which is included in his signature on these screens.
Matsushima (Pine Islands) is the name of a famous site (meisho) near Sendai, a city in Miyagi Prefecture in northeastern Japan. Its beauty inspired both poets and painters. In Sotatsu's screens the rock from which pine trees grow are rendered in brilliant mineral colors of green, blue, and brown, highlighted with gold. Waves in animated forms are delineated in alternating lines of ink and gold, producing a luminous effect. Clouds and embankments are rendered in particles of gold leaf accented with silver, which has darkened over time to a soft black tone.
Sotatsu's innovative composition creates a dynamic interplay among the land and cloud forms, the bending pines, and churning waves. His lifelong interest in pictorial composition left a lasting legacy. Later painters of the Rimpa school, such as Ogata Korin (1658-1716), repeated the Matsushima theme in their work. The Matsushima screens were probably painted in the early Kan'ei era (1624-44), a period of cultural efflorescence in Kyoto.

CuratorialText: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in linear relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically excavated in China, so it is difficult to know the purpose of this extremely rare axe. It consists of four separate pieces: a tubular bronze handle, two caps with dragon motifs, and an unusual serrated jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was lengthened with additional elements that are now missing.
lexical_adapted: Ceremonial sickle in four parts: three of bronze inlaid with turquoise, one (the blade) of jade decorated in line-like relief with notched back and ground edge. No objects of this type have yet been scientifically dug up in China, so it is hard to know the purpose of this very rare axe. It is made of four separate pieces: a tube-shaped bronze handle, two caps with dragon designs, and an unusual jagged jade blade that might not be original. Traces of wood inside the handle suggest it was made longer with added parts that are now missing.

CuratorialText: This is one of a pair of folding screens illustrating two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a narrative composed in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This fictional biography of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a frequent subject of Japanese paintings. This screen represents chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, drift together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily isolated in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose monochromatic ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more commonly illustrated in lavish color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically evocative of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.
lexical_adapted: This is one of a pair of folding screens showing two of the fifty-four chapters in The Tale of Genji, a story written in the early eleventh century by a lady of the court known as Murasaki Shikibu. This made-up life story of Prince Genji, whose life was filled with romance and tragedy, was a common subject of Japanese paintings. This screen shows chapter fifty-one, Ukifune. The lady Ukifune and her lover Niou, float together in a boat on a wintry day, sheltered by a folding screen and temporarily alone in their tragic romance. The painter Kano Naonobu chose black-and-white ink and thin color washes for a theme that was more often shown in rich color and gold. The dreamlike effect of the mists and washes is poetically suggestive of the mood of each episode. Naonobu was an official painter who served the Tokugawa shogun.

CuratorialText: Ornament of roughly rectangular shape; translucent mottled tan and cream color with areas of white film of incipient disintegration; blunt blade edge is indicated by lightly incised line and beveling; upper edge has irregular notches and a narrow projection; satin smooth surface. (One small chip). Acquired with a box, now lost.

CuratorialText: Jar, large, ovoidal, on a low, thick foot rim. Clay: soft, grayish. Glaze: variable aubergine, frosted with whitish areas of partial disintegration, with a slight iridescence. Earthy attachments. Decoration: impressed, incised and modelled in the paste.

CuratorialText: Bowl: deep, ovoidal; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, mottled with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.
lexical_adapted: Bowl: deep, egg-shaped; low foot. Clay: hard, white. Glaze: pale green, spotted with blue-violet inside; gray-olive on rim and edge of flow; finely pitted.

CuratorialText: Chinese Han Glazed pottery " Hill-jar"; cylindrical vessel with cover; three bear-shaped feet; spur marks. Clay: reddish, sandy texture; fired medium. Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery iridescence, earth incrustations. Decoration: horizontal panels moulded in relief with animals a nd horsemen; two simulated mask and ring handles; cover represents mountains with animals in relief.

Glaze: lead silicate; deep green with areas of silvery shine, earth crusts. CuratorialText: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most beloved and influential artists. A true titan of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual heft and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such profound success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was regarded as the pinnacle of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first comprehensive exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to illuminate the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael engaged with the foremost writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, displaying a poetic sensibility that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full breadth of his life and career, from his origins in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as a peer of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, prolific decade at the papal court in Rome. To underscore the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With particular attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare opportunity to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.
lexical_adapted: Dive into the artistic process of one of history’s most loved and influential artists. A true giant of the Italian Renaissance, Raffaello di Giovanni Santi (1483–1520)—better known as Raphael—matched ambition with lyricism to create works with both intellectual weight and emotional depth, a necessary skill in the complex political landscape of Renaissance courts. In his short life of only 37 years, he achieved such great success as a painter, designer, and architect that he was seen as the highest point of artistic perfection for centuries after his death. Raphael: Sublime Poetry is the first full exhibition on Raphael in the United States, bringing together more than 170 of the artist’s greatest masterpieces and rarely seen treasures to show the brilliance of Raphael’s extraordinary creativity. The son of a painter and poet, Raphael worked with the leading writers and thinkers of his age in Rome, showing a poetic sensitivity that captivated his peers and generations that followed. Follow the full range of his life and career, from his beginnings in Urbino to his rise in Florence, where he began to emerge as an equal of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, to his final, productive decade at the papal court in Rome. To highlight the range of his genius, this presentation brings together important drawings, paintings, and tapestries from public and private collections across Europe and the United States, many of which have never been shown together. With special attention to Raphael’s portrayal of women—from his use of nude female models for the first time in Western art to his tender depictions of the Madonna and Child—and recent scientific discoveries made using state-of-the-art technology, this exhibition offers a rare chance to experience the genius of an artist who helped shape the course of art history.

CuratorialText: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative facility, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of assistants and collaborators to propagate his inventions and style, spawning a new generation of noteworthy artists.
lexical_adapted: One of the greatest influencers of all time, Raphael (1483 –1520) was idolized for more than three centuries, in Europe and beyond, as the painter of supreme perfection and the role model for artists in training. During his short life, his contemporaries celebrated his enormous creative ability, and later his fame towered above that of other Italian Renaissance stars. Born Raffaello di Giovanni Santi, the ambitious young artist soon left behind his modest origins in the Marche region of central Italy in search of important patrons in Umbria and Tuscany. In Florence, then the capital of the European art world, he emerged as a peer to the elder Leonardo and Michelangelo. Raphael spent his final decade as the favorite artist of the popes in Rome, where he was praised as the “prince of painters.” A savvy businessman and impresario, he worked with well-organized teams of helpers and coworkers to spread his inventions and style, creating a new generation of noteworthy artists.

CuratorialText: This first comprehensive U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an immersive look at his meteoric career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view present a portrait of his dashing artistic personality and astonishingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became an intimate friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at composing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture accustomed to thinking of painting and poetry as intertwined sister arts. The elegance and dramatic force of his imagery still have the power to invoke the ancient dictum, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a mute poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”
lexical_adapted: This first complete U.S. exhibition on Raphael provides an deep look at his fast-moving career. Together, the drawings, paintings, prints, and tapestries on view show a picture of his charming artistic personality and amazingly creative mind. This is also the story of an artist who was born to a poet-painter, became a close friend to literary figures, and tried his hand at writing sonnets. Raphael lived and worked in a culture used to thinking of painting and poetry as connected sister arts. The grace and strong force of his images still have the power to call up the old saying, much discussed in his time, that “painting is a silent poetry, and poetry is a blind painting.”

CuratorialText: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.
lexical_adapted: Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, a hill town set in a spectacular, rugged landscape in the Marche region, in east central Italy. The artist’s roots in Urbino, with its refined court life and unique intellectual heritage, played a defining role in his life and career. He proudly identified with his birthplace and signed his paintings “RAPHAEL VRBINAS” to the end of his days.

CuratorialText: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and prolific poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy composing an epic poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A formidable general, scholar, and patron of art and architecture, Federico transformed his court into an internationally celebrated center for the arts and humanist learning. He founded a fabled library and rebuilt Urbino’s imposing Palazzo Ducale, whose rhythmic arcades and harmonic geometry embodied the humanist dedication to mathematics that distinguished the culture of Urbino. Those same ideals are evident in the vibrant architectural visions—enlivened with perspectival effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.
lexical_adapted: Raphael received his earliest training from his father, Giovanni Santi (ca. 1439–1494), a court painter and productive poet. During Raphael’s childhood, Giovanni was busy writing a long poem on Federico III da Montefeltro (1422–1482), the famous ruler who had brought peace, wealth, and fame to the Duchy of Urbino. A powerful general, scholar, and supporter of art and building, Federico turned his court into a world-famous center for the arts and humanist learning. He started a legendary library and rebuilt Urbino’s impressive Palazzo Ducale, whose flowing arcades and balanced geometry showed the humanist dedication to math that set apart the culture of Urbino. Those same ideas are clear in the lively building views—made lively with depth effects and classical references—created by Urbino painters.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s father soon brought his precocious son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, prolific painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on collaboration with assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael coursed quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a collaborator to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, breathtaking command of technique, and efficient methods for reproducing designs left their mark on the younger artist.
lexical_adapted: Raphael’s father soon brought his talented son to study with Pietro Perugino (1446/50–1523), an artist he likely knew through local projects. A brilliant, productive painter and draftsman with active workshops in Perugia and Florence, Perugino relied heavily on help from assistants to meet the demands of patrons all over Italy. Raphael moved quickly through the stages of pupil and apprentice to become first a helper to Perugino and then an independent artist. Perugino’s elegant figures, amazing command of technique, and efficient methods for copying designs left their mark on the younger artist.

CuratorialText: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious institutions for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of patronage for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.
lexical_adapted: Ambitious, studious, and disciplined, the young Raphael first sought to establish himself as an independent professional in Città di Castello, a small, wealthy town near Perugia, where he painted notable church altarpieces. Confraternities, religious groups for regular citizens devoted to works of charity, were also major sources of support for artists in Umbria and the Marche. Gathered here are paintings and related studies done for confraternities by Perugino and the young Raphael—including the first painting fully in Raphael’s hand, as confirmed by its recent conservation treatment.

CuratorialText: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael endeavored to cultivate patrons by painting both monumental altarpieces and small-scale devotional works in the regions of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. An iconic example is the large, multipart painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full ensemble is reunited here as a complete work for the first time since it was dismembered around 1663 and the individual paintings dispersed. Other notable projects include a mammoth altarpiece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and survives only in fragments and preliminary drawings, and a remarkable altarpiece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, represented here by studies and the painting that provided its base.
lexical_adapted: From 1500 to 1507, the young Raphael worked to win over patrons by painting both large altar pieces and small religious works in the areas of the Marche, Umbria, and Tuscany. A classic example is the large, multi-part painting at the center of this gallery, made for nuns in Perugia and now known as the Colonna Altarpiece (after a later owner). The full set is brought together here as a complete work for the first time since it was taken apart around 1663 and the individual paintings spread out. Other important projects include a huge altar piece for the Baronci family chapel in Città di Castello, which was later destroyed in an earthquake and exists only in pieces and early drawings, and a remarkable altar piece for the Oddi family chapel in Perugia, shown here by studies and the painting that formed its base.

CuratorialText: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece reveal the workshop practices that Raphael had absorbed during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Dazzling studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of media—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a virtuosic command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and undoubtedly impressed his patrons.
lexical_adapted: Full-scale drawings for the Oddi Altarpiece show the workshop practices that Raphael had learned during his training and collaboration with Perugino. Stunning studies on paper also show how he worked in a variety of materials—black chalk, pen and ink, and metalpoint on prepared paper—often with a masterful command of technique. Drawings such as these likely served as his calling card and certainly impressed his patrons.

CuratorialText: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular murals there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery convey the electrifying novelty and arresting sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, promptly and seamlessly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.
lexical_adapted: Raphael may have been drawn to Florence after hearing fellow painters rave about rival full-scale designs (cartoons) for spectacular wall paintings there by Leonardo and Michelangelo. Related works in this gallery show the exciting newness and striking sculptural power of those unrealized projects. While in Florence between 1504 and 1508, Raphael also studied Michelangelo’s sculptures and Leonardo’s paintings and drawings, quickly and smoothly adapting aspects of their styles and techniques in his work.

CuratorialText: Raphael also learned from his peers’ revolutionary approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s theories about “sketching quickly,” in the fervor of artistic inspiration, transformed Raphael’s methods. Asserting that the effort to finish drawings and puzzle out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet scrawling the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more artisanal way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more organic compositions pulsating with movement and vivid expression.
lexical_adapted: Raphael also learned from his peers’ new approaches to inventing compositions. Leonardo’s ideas about “sketching quickly,” in the heat of artistic inspiration, changed Raphael’s methods. Saying that the effort to finish drawings and work out details could dry up fresh ideas, Leonardo advised working out concepts in “rough sketches,” like a poet writing the first draft of a verse in messy handwriting with crossed-out words. Before Florence, Raphael had tended to develop compositions in a more craft-based way, figure by detailed figure, as he had learned from Perugino. Now, spontaneous sketching led him to more natural compositions full of movement and vivid expression.

CuratorialText: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art infused Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of an enormous amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—reveal Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually animated a pyramidal composition (favored by Leonardo), which could seem static, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and gazes among the figures.
lexical_adapted: The exposure to Leonardo’s and Michelangelo’s art filled Raphael’s compositions with a sense of space, sculptural monumentality, and expressive force. This was the result of a huge amount of experimentation on paper. Sequences of drawings—from a sheet of sketches to studies of an arrangement based on three-dimensional clay or wax models—show Raphael’s increasingly disciplined approach to the design process. Working in orderly steps, for example, he gradually brought to life a pyramidal composition (liked by Leonardo), which could seem still, by building a dynamic interplay of gestures and looks among the figures.

CuratorialText: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most significant work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s entombment for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The preliminary drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and demonstrate how he constructed a dramatic composition with a climactic narrative. Through arduous study, aided by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist evolved a static arrangement of figures into an emotionally arresting action scene. Raphael had absorbed Leonardo’s principle that the physical gestures of the body express the “motions” of the mind and soul.
lexical_adapted: In 1507 the twenty-four-year-old Raphael painted his most important work before his move to Rome, an altarpiece of Christ’s burial for the Baglioni family chapel in Perugia. The early drawings show him in full command of his powers as a storyteller, and show how he built a dramatic composition with a peak narrative. Through hard study, helped by his knowledge of classical sculpture, the artist changed a still arrangement of figures into an emotionally gripping action scene. Raphael had taken in Leonardo’s idea that the physical gestures of the body express the “movements” of the mind and soul.

CuratorialText: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a display of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary embraces the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Disseminated widely, the imagery inspired numerous medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and thoroughly transformed it.
lexical_adapted: For centuries Raphael has been widely admired for Madonna and Child paintings that include a show of tenderness. From about 1300, Byzantine icons of the Eastern Orthodox faith had featured a form of the Madonna known as the Virgin Eleousa, from the Greek for “compassionate,” in which Mary holds the Christ Child as he touches his cheek to hers. Spread widely, the imagery inspired many medieval and Renaissance artists, including Italian sculptors and painters. Raphael drew upon this model and completely changed it.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of vitality and innocent playfulness, especially while maintaining a superior command of anatomical realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book displayed here records funerary expenses for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related complications in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of gynecology and obstetrics was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant mortality rates astronomically high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to humanize religious subjects, all contributed to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as votive figures and subjects in art.
lexical_adapted: Raphael’s ability to capture a mood of life and innocent playfulness, especially while keeping a superior command of body realism, is striking in light of the historical realities of childbirth, infancy, and parenthood in his time. The rare account book shown here records funeral costs for Raphael’s mother, who died of childbirth-related problems in 1491, and for two of his siblings who died in infancy. Knowledge of women’s health and childbirth was scarce, pregnancy and childbirth extremely dangerous, and infant death rates very high. These facts, along with a desire among Renaissance artists to make religious subjects more human, all added to the appeal of the tender Madonna and Child as prayer figures and subjects in art.

CuratorialText: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely thwarted in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most feasible source of patronage among the city’s wealthy merchants, who commissioned portraits and tender devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his collaborators followed his example.
lexical_adapted: Despite his great ambitions, Raphael was largely blocked in his attempts to break into the competitive art market in Florence. He found his most workable source of support among the city’s wealthy merchants, who ordered portraits and gentle devotional paintings, mainly of the Madonna and Child. Raphael continued to produce works on this subject for private patrons well after his move to Rome in 1508, and many of his coworkers followed his example.

CuratorialText: Raphael sought to bring the lauded ethereal physical beauty of the Madonna into radiant being. He was not immune to the convention, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, aristocratic lady with dainty facial features and golden hair, but he also strove for long-established Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as mirrors of her unique spiritual perfection. He infused his representations with a humanity and psychological presence revealed through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings display an arresting command of light, color, space, and geometry, and his renderings of infants are more naturalistic in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his peers.
lexical_adapted: Raphael sought to bring the praised heavenly physical beauty of the Madonna into shining being. He was not free from the custom, sung by poets both within and beyond his circle, that favored an elegant, upper-class lady with delicate facial features and golden hair, but he also aimed for long-standing Christian ideals. Sermons, prayers, songs, and poetry inspired by early church writings had praised the Virgin’s beauty and grace as reflections of her unique spiritual perfection. He filled his pictures with a humanity and mental presence shown through gestures and reactions. Raphael’s paintings show an impressive control of light, color, space, and geometry, and his images of babies are more true-to-life in their proportions, poses, and expressions than those of many of his equals.

CuratorialText: Raphael’s portraits communicate a profound empathy and reflect years of exercising his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, concerted contemplation of the sitter. The regal pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a revolutionary model for the likeness of a luxuriously dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, torso erect, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the cultivated manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty celebrated by famous poets. Raphael was an intimate friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of comportment and grace that prized sprezzatura, a studied nonchalance or insouciance.
lexical_adapted: Raphael’s portraits show a deep empathy and reflect years of practicing his hand in drawings to achieve an intimate, focused study of the sitter. The majestic pose of Leonardo’s Mona Lisa, begun in 1503, offered a groundbreaking model for the likeness of a richly dressed person: a three-quarter view, head turned to the left, body upright, and hands resting gracefully at the waist. The elegance of the poses here also suggests a striving for the refined manners of the Italian Renaissance courts and the ideals of beauty praised by famous poets. Raphael was a close friend of Baldassarre Castiglione (1478–1529), whose book on fashionable behavior at court promoted a model of conduct and grace that valued sprezzatura, a studied casualness or ease.

CuratorialText: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of betrothal and marriage, a financial-dynastic arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Overt appraisals of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.
lexical_adapted: Many Renaissance portraits of women were painted at the time of engagement and marriage, a financial-family arrangement in which romantic love had no place. Elegant fashion and jewelry were meant to impress the future husband’s family, as dowries and property were negotiated. Open judgments of women’s appearances by poets and artists also contributed to a culture in which young women could become innamorate—platonic beloveds or actual mistresses—of a male aristocracy.

CuratorialText: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An intriguing question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to supersede an earlier generation of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal savvy were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also paved the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.
lexical_adapted: At some point in 1508, Raphael arrived in Rome, where he would become the favorite court artist of Popes Julius II (r. 1503–13) and Leo X (r. 1513–21). An interesting question is how exactly the twenty-five-year-old Raphael managed to overtake an earlier group of painters working in the Vatican Palace and take control of the fresco decoration of the most important of the rooms, the Stanza della Segnatura. Surely artistic genius and personal skill were key factors, but research shows that networks of political influence between Rome and his hometown of Urbino also cleared the way, including support from his friend and countryman Donato Bramante (1444–1514), the pope’s favorite architect.

CuratorialText: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing media. Rapidly sketched pen-and-ink sheets teem with the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear monumental despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video conveys the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.
lexical_adapted: Gathered here are Raphael’s studies for those Vatican frescoes: the School of Athens, with its gathering of philosophers, and the Disputa, representing Roman Catholic theology. These works show an artist in full command of the expressive potential of different drawing materials. Quickly sketched pen-and-ink sheets are full of the energy of ideas in the making, while studies in black chalk appear large and impressive despite their small size. In the next gallery, a digital video shows the power of Raphael’s finished frescoes in four rooms of the Vatican Palace.

CuratorialText: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting accolades as the most visible and prolific painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo labored to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he concluded his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X ascended to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.
lexical_adapted: By about 1510 Raphael was collecting praise as the most visible and productive painter at the papal court in Rome; meanwhile, shut away in the Sistine Chapel, the secretive Michelangelo worked to paint his ceiling frescoes, which would deeply influence the younger artist, who snuck in to see them. Drawings here relate to projects that occupied Raphael as he finished his first series of frescoes in the Vatican Palace and began the second, in the Stanza di Eliodoro, which communicate with greater monumentality and dramatic force. After Pope Julius II died in 1513 and Leo X rose to the throne, some learned imagery vanished in favor of blunter papal messaging.

CuratorialText: Other drawings and prints illustrate Raphael’s collaboration with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their fruitful partnership, beginning in 1510, they created engravings of tremendous technical skill and artistic power. This medium enabled the reproduction of imagery in multiples and ensured a long afterlife for Raphael’s compositions. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and repurposed in other media, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.
lexical_adapted: Other drawings and prints show Raphael’s work with Marcantonio Raimondi (ca. 1480–1534), a Bolognese printmaker and close friend. In their successful partnership, starting in 1510, they made engravings of great technical skill and artistic power. This method allowed the copying of images in many copies and gave a long life for Raphael’s works. The prints were copied by artists well into the late 1800s and used again in other materials, such as the maiolica plates in a nearby case.

CuratorialText: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s monumental fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of scale and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the gargantuan oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).
lexical_adapted: In the next gallery is a digital video showing Raphael’s large fresco cycles in four rooms of the Vatican Palace. The display provides a sense of size and context for the many related drawings in this exhibition. Between 1508 and 1524, the frescoes were painted by Raphael and his workshop in the nearly square rooms now known as the Stanza della Segnatura, Stanza di Eliodoro, and Stanza dell’Incendio, and in the huge oblong Sala di Costantino (completed by his assistants after his death in 1520).

CuratorialText: The durable but difficult fresco technique requires expertise, the use of full-scale drawings (cartoons), and a willingness to anticipate challenges.
