The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
lexical_adapted: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a railing, holding a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
lexical_adapted: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round base, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his playfully smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the container lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round base. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The container filled with arrows shows, among other things, grooved decoration, a ring of laurel leaves, and curved grooves. The pedestal, a short column, has a shaped base decorated with a ring of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved grooves containing leaf-shaped ornaments transitioning into a smooth upper section broken by a shaped edge, and furthermore, a shaped, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart pattern, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
lexical_adapted: This portrait of Alida Assink shows her in her finest clothes, shown from head to toe. That is quite unusual, as the painter Kruseman usually kept such a full-length portrait only for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this picture was ordered by her guardian, a rich landowner. The country setting with hunting dog and garden vase goes back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
lexical_adapted: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird world. His keen powers of observation are shown by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape lightly painted in clear shades of green and blue brings to mind the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
lexical_adapted: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
lexical_adapted: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, worked for greater independence and supported freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was killed four years later.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
lexical_adapted: This is a wide, sun-filled river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with roughly drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers stop at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This peaceful scene does not take place in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
lexical_adapted: Ice fun near a village. Winter village scene with many people skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
lexical_adapted: This dolls’ house is very realistic. All the contents have been made of real materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
lexical_adapted: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to careful and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
lexical_adapted: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The event was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the city guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum announces the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can from now on be laid to rest.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
lexical_adapted: The Fishing for Souls: a story of the jealousy of the different religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river scene, large groups of people are placed on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their backers: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the young Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the front sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their backers: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
lexical_adapted: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being raised, the anchors weighed. The back of the ship on the right in the front is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat with important figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last supplies and crew are still being taken on board. The huge accuracy in the showing of details—ropes, sails, and cannons—is striking.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
lexical_adapted: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), likely shown as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.

lexical_adapted: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to stop Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's arrow holder hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.

lexical_adapted: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with loose sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with folded sails. Pair to SK-A-1848.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
lexical_adapted: The word Sufi often brings up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
lexical_adapted: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts filled with Sufi meanings. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked openly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and region. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and self-denial, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
lexical_adapted: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a stunning celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved gods, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition remembers over 200 years since this unlucky royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of working with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on native knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition shows seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
lexical_adapted: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its link to today's society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his work grew to include the making of prints and three-dimensional artworks in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he made with the help of various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, creating a thought garden at his faraway home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which included many of his artworks. He named his garden Little Sparta after the old Greek city, a military rival to Athens, showing his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
lexical_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and breaks, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's involvement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints given in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written a lot on Finlay and who wrote letters to the artist for many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
lexical_adapted: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A student of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by copying the genre scenes that won Greuze fame at the Salons of the 1760s, showing a liking for delicacy that suited the tastes of the old order.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
lexical_adapted: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he kept himself busy recording the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more simple taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was known more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's gift shows Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented artist whose works offer a fascinating look into French society before, during and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
lexical_adapted: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was taken by British forces and later bought by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display brings together these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and meaning in West Africa. Its location in the center of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of important cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had very different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
lexical_adapted: This show is part of a three-year study project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, looking into the links between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close teamwork with coworkers at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
lexical_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic new ideas. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
lexical_adapted: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition shows the growth of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional range and quality of the British Museum's collection gives a unique chance to present a full account of drawing from this area. Early Netherlandish drawings are very rare, as they were made as useful objects in the workshop and were not usually kept. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this time. Drawings were central to the design and making of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and teamwork of Netherlandish artists.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
lexical_adapted: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they come directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
lexical_adapted: Be among the first to see designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
lexical_adapted: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft base, the memorial in St James' Park will honor the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be finished in 2028. Designed by famous sculptor Martin Jennings, to be carved in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
lexical_adapted: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving husband of a queen, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year rule. Other parts of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display shows small models of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister looked at the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a head and shoulders sculpture of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
lexical_adapted: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for rest and thought. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for renewing community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
lexical_adapted:
Discover how people lived and died in old Egypt and explore their hopes and dreams for the life after death. 
From human remains, painted coffins, charms, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to amazing wall paintings and papyri; look into what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of old Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the dead for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an endless restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the life after death. It will also show how funeral remains can tell us about the lives of the old Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to caring for human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our rules governing the holding, display and study of human remains.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
lexical_adapted:
Discover the interesting history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most famous spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, huge tomb architecture and ancient tomb carvings spanning 3,000 years. These include the imposing large bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
lexical_adapted: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The nearby galleries also explore parts of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and sorted. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest shows the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
lexical_adapted: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will stay on public display for generations to come. In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel linked to Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly skilled jewel with enamel designs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a hanging clasp in the form of a hand coming out of a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the deeply Catholic Katherine, a result of the ending of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it strongly shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
lexical_adapted: In the 18th century, the collections were split into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural examples from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his plant collection. In 1735, the Swedish plant expert Carl Linnaeus changed the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and grouped. His student, Daniel Solander, was a keeper in the Museum and used the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history examples Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original examples are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history examples that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
lexical_adapted: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and objects from the past, and traveled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these items had survived, including religious objects, armor, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and methodical way and to learn more about the earliest Britons. This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock layers, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
lexical_adapted: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman statues, bought on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical old items from fellow experts such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'simple' beginnings in early cultures to what they considered to be the peak of artistic success – the statues and buildings of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
lexical_adapted: Everything gathered in the 1700s had to be classified and organized so that it could be more easily understood and used. King George III's library was given to the nation by George IV and was kept in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected tools and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, trying to set up a timeline for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated books. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were listed and shown in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
lexical_adapted: The Enlightenment led to a strong interest in decoding the mysterious shapes of Egyptian picture-writing and the wedge-shaped marks found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, history experts decoded runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when large numbers of ancient objects, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through digging, entered the British Museum.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
lexical_adapted: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars tried to clear this up by studying the ancient and modern religions they met. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious objects, grouped gods into families and looked at their qualities and roles. They wanted to find similarities that might explain the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
lexical_adapted: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected objects and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European viewpoint. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through workers of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labor and the taking advantage of people and resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
lexical_adapted: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles. The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major building programs and were great supporters of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a huge carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
lexical_adapted: The exhibition is a bright celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together amazing objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved gods, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across huge oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition honors over 200 years since this unlucky royal visit and shows light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complicated and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
